citations: Hertel et al. (1997) GTAP model documentation — top-level Armington elasticities typically in 2–4 range for aggregate goods
Feenstra (1994, REStat) — estimates of import elasticities for U.S. manufacturing, central estimates often 2–3
McDaniel & Balistreri (2003, Economic Modelling) — survey of Armington elasticity estimates, noting wide range but modal values near 2–3 for aggregate U.S. trade
runs.reasoningSummary: The top-level Armington elasticity (import composite vs. domestic good) in CGE models calibrated to U.S. data is conventionally set between 2 and 4, with many standard models (e.g., GTAP, USITC models) using values around 2–3. Econometric estimates such as Feenstra (1994) and subsequent work suggest central values near 2–3 for aggregate U.S. trade flows, though estimates vary considerably across sectors and methods. Some macro-oriented estimates are lower (near 1–1.5), while sector-level structural estimates can reach 5+. I center my median at 2.5, reflecting the most common calibration values in the literature, with meaningful uncertainty across the 1–5.5 range.

runs.reasoningSummary: The top-level Armington elasticity between aggregate imports and domestic goods in U.S. CGE models is conventionally set around 2, consistent with the Armington (1969) framework and empirical estimates. I place the median at 2.0 with moderate uncertainty, allowing for values as low as ~0.8 (very inelastic sectors or short-run estimates) and as high as ~5 (long-run macro or demand-system estimates).
